text:
I want to relate, by way of a brief narrative, the past expedition our association mounted under the auspice of a major university. Our goal was the cemetery archives of an ancient site in Egypt, where an honorable scholar believed a miracle of preservation lay buried. I packed my briefcase and a portable filing kit, never imagining how a single drive across the desert would change my view of politics, of posterity, and of myself.

The lead researcher, a smart and obliging man, was no timid bachelor of theory; he had real conceptive power. "All of us," he said, "carry the onus of honest work. Do not exaggerate, do not generalize, and never defer a hard question." His brother-in-law, a creditworthy consumer-goods broker, had funded the trip, but the man refused to let money impose on the science. When a glut of cheap rumors threatened to infest the project, he would simply slam the briefcase shut and stride out to clear his head.

The work was almost pure chore. We would bore into packed earth, tow equipment by motor across the lot, and grab any fragment the net of our sieve caught. A faint wavelength on the metric scanner, equivalent to a heartbeat, made the whole machine vibrate. Algae, deuterium traces, kerosene lamps, even an old palm-sized trophy of bronze emerged from the dark. Each fleeting find felt like regeneration of a lost world. I learned to persevere through exasperation, to ventilate the tomb so no one fainted, and to file every chart faithfully.

Incidentally, the awkwardness of our first week gave way to something fashionable among the team: a quiet pride. A westerner on the crew, once prone to misunderstanding the locals, grew to respect them; an unkind remark would now earn a glide of disapproving silence. We learned that voting on every decision, however restless it made us, kept the autonomy of the group intact and warded off aggression. The proceeds of the dig, the jewelry and the records, were never ours to own; they belonged to Egypt and to posterity. As the silver dawn rose over the desert and a stray dog trotted past my desk in the garage-turned-office, I sat at the garage table and wrote it all down, sure that this dishonor-free, faithfully kept account would outlast us all. On Friday the team would gather for a preparatory seminar, a relaxed news briefing where each volunteer could dilate on the week and not lag behind. We treated experimental methods and old methods equally, attentive to every nuance; a sample of alga from the tomb wall, we agreed, held no exclusivity over a coin. When the season ended, the locals gave us a warm ovation we would never forget.
translation:
我想以一段简短的叙述，讲述一下我们协会在一所重点大学的赞助下进行的那次过往的考察。我们的目标是埃及一处古迹的墓地档案，一位可敬的学者相信那里埋藏着一个保存完好的奇迹。我收拾好公文包和一套便携的档案管理工具，从未想过一次横穿沙漠的驾驶会如何改变我对政治、对后代、以及对自己的看法。

那位首席研究员是个聪明而热心助人的人，绝非纸上谈兵、胆怯的理论单身汉；他有真正的构想力。“我们所有人，”他说，“都肩负着诚实工作的责任。不要夸大，不要笼统概括，也绝不要推迟一个棘手的问题。”他的姻兄是位信誉良好的消费品经纪人，资助了这趟旅程，但他拒绝让金钱强加于科学之上。当大量廉价的谣言滋生、威胁到这个项目时，他只会砰地合上公文包，大步走出去清醒头脑。

这工作几乎纯是杂活。我们要钻进压实的泥土，用马达拖着设备穿过场地，抓取筛网捕到的任何碎片。公制扫描仪上一道微弱的波长，相当于一次心跳，会让整台机器震动起来。藻类、氘的痕迹、煤油灯，甚至一座古老的、手掌大小的青铜奖品，都从黑暗中浮现。每一个转瞬即逝的发现都像失落世界的再生。我学会了在愤懑中坚持，学会给墓室通风以免有人晕倒，并忠实地归档每一张图表。

顺便说一句，我们第一周的笨拙渐渐让位于团队中一种时髦的东西：一种安静的自豪。队里一位西方人，曾经容易误解当地人，后来逐渐尊重他们；如今一句不和善的话会换来一阵不以为然的沉默滑过。我们懂得了：对每个决定进行投票，尽管让我们焦躁不安，却维护了团队的自治，并防止了侵略性的争斗。发掘的收益——那些珠宝和记录——从来都不归我们所有；它们属于埃及，属于后代。当银色的黎明在沙漠上升起，一条野狗从我那设在车库改成的办公室里的书桌旁小跑而过，我坐在车库的桌前把这一切都写了下来，确信这份没有耻辱、忠实保存的记录将比我们所有人都更长久。每逢周五，团队会聚在一起开一次预备研讨会，那是一场轻松的新闻通报会，每位志愿者都可以就这一周畅谈一番，不至于落后。我们对实验性方法和旧方法一视同仁，留心每一处细微差别；我们一致认为，墓壁上的一份藻类样本并不比一枚硬币更具独家价值。当这一季结束时，当地人给了我们一阵永生难忘的热烈欢呼。
